version_history.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/10] nfsd: fix BUG_ON in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid on racing delegation revoke
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a race condition in the NFSv4 server (nfsd) where a concurrent delegation revoke can cause a BUG_ON when trying to read fi_deleg_file without holding the corresponding lock. The fix involves taking the spinlock around the fi_deleg_file read and replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error return.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the spinlock usage, suggesting it may not be necessary due to the recent commit that added a stability guarantee. Also pointed out a potential issue with rcu_dereference_protected() and suggested adding a comment.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 29 May 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid reads fp->fi_deleg_file without holding
> fi_lock when the parent stateid is a delegation. A concurrent delegation
> revoke via the laundromat can clear fi_deleg_file under fi_lock, causing
> nfsd_file_get() to return NULL and triggering the BUG_ON.
> 
> This race is client-reachable: two NFS clients can trigger it by having
> one hold a delegation while another opens the same file to force a
> recall. When the first client doesn't respond to the recall, the
> laundromat revokes it. A concurrent LAYOUTGET from any client using the
> delegation stateid hits the race window.
> 
> Fix this by taking fi_lock around the fi_deleg_file read in the
> SC_TYPE_DELEG path, and replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error
> return that cleans up the partially-initialized layout stateid.

Replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error is certainly sensible and
probably all that is needed to fix the problem.

I cannot see how the spinlock achieves anything.  If ->fi_deleg_file
could become NULL at this point, it can become NULL just before we take
the spinlock.

We do need to be sure the file (if there is one) doesn't get freed while
nfsd_file_get() is incrementing the refcount, but rcu_read_lock() is the
normal tool for that.
In this case we have

  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));

rcu_dereference_protected(...., 1)
which for me is a warning sign.  What does the '1' mean here?
Presumably something that we cannot easily assert with a C condition, in
which case a comment is called for.

Based on the recent commit which added this I'm guessing 
   fi_delegees > 0 guarantees stability

If that is right, then why do we also need a spinlock to guarantee
stability?

Confused.

NeilBrown

> 
> Fixes: c5c707f96fc9 ("nfsd: implement pNFS layout recalls")
> Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c | 12 +++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> index 9ed2e3d65062..5d48c7673fa1 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4layouts.c
> @@ -247,11 +247,17 @@ nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid(struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
>  	nfsd4_init_cb(&ls->ls_recall, clp, &nfsd4_cb_layout_ops,
>  			NFSPROC4_CLNT_CB_LAYOUT);
>  
> -	if (parent->sc_type == SC_TYPE_DELEG)
> +	if (parent->sc_type == SC_TYPE_DELEG) {
> +		spin_lock(&fp->fi_lock);
>  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));
> -	else
> +		spin_unlock(&fp->fi_lock);
> +	} else {
>  		ls->ls_file = find_any_file(fp);
> -	BUG_ON(!ls->ls_file);
> +	}
> +	if (!ls->ls_file) {
> +		nfs4_put_stid(stp);
> +		return NULL;
> +	}
>  
>  	ls->ls_fenced = false;
>  	ls->ls_fence_delay = 0;
> 
> -- 
> 2.54.0

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/10] nfsd: drain callbacks and clear cl_cb_session
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server (nfsd) where a session is freed while an RPC task still holds a reference to it, causing a use-after-free error. The patch introduces a two-stage drain mechanism in nfsd4_probe_callback_sync() to wait for all RPC tasks to complete before freeing the session. Additionally, clp->cl_cb_session is cleared unconditionally on error returns from nfsd4_process_cb_update(). This ensures that the NFS server properly handles session teardown and callback processing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/10] nfsd: serialize nfsd4_end_grace() with atomic test-and-set
series_items.patch_summary: This patch serializes the nfsd4_end_grace() function by using an atomic test-and-set operation to ensure that only one thread can transition the grace_ended flag from false to true at a time, preventing concurrent access and corruption of the reclaim_str_hashtbl list.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/10] nfsd: dedup nfs4_client_to_reclaim inserts
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 client recovery mechanism where duplicate entries were being inserted into the reclaim hash table, leading to incorrect behavior during the grace period. The fix involves computing the string hash value first and then looking up the name in the hash table. If an existing entry is found, the new principal hash is folded into the existing record without allocating a new entry or modifying the hash table size. This ensures that the fast end-of-grace path works correctly and prevents dangling entries from being left in the hash table.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/10] nfsd: gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd3_proc_setacl function to gate NFSv3 setacl operations based on the argp->mask value. The function previously called set_posix_acl unconditionally for both ACL_TYPE_ACCESS and ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, but now checks the corresponding mask bit before setting the ACLs. This change ensures that a SETACL with mask=NFS_ACL silently drops the directory's default ACL, and a request with neither bit set leaves the on-disk ACLs untouched.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/10] NFSD: Enable return of an updated stable_how to NFS clients
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables the return of an updated stable_how value to NFS clients, which will be used in a subsequent patch to promote UNSTABLE WRITEs to FILE_SYNC WRITEs, reducing round trips and allowing clients to discard cached dirty data. The patch refactors nfsd_vfs_write() to return a possibly modified stable_how value without changing its behavior. It affects several files in the NFS server code, including nfs3proc.c, nfs4proc.c, nfsproc.c, vfs.c, and xdr3.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/10] NFSD: check truncate permission under inode lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a TOCTOU (Time-of-Check, Time-of-Use) vulnerability in the NFSD code by ensuring that truncate permission checks are performed under inode lock. The patch splits the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check and rechecks whether the requested size is below the locked file size after acquiring the inode lock. This change prevents concurrent appends from extending the file before the NFSD MAY_TRUNC check, which rejects append-only files. The patch also avoids loading the unlocked inode->i_size value.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/10] nfsd: fix partial-write detection in nfsd_direct_write
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_direct_write function, which is responsible for handling direct writes to NFS servers. The issue was that partial-write detection was not working correctly, allowing short writes to complete between 50% and 99% of the segment size without breaking the loop. This caused the next segment's payload to be written at the wrong offset and over-reporting the count to the NFS client. To fix this, a snapshot of the segment's byte count is taken before writing, and host_err is compared against this snapshot instead of the original request length. This ensures that any short write breaks the loop as expected.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/10] nfsd: cap decoded POSIX ACL count to bound sort cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents a potential denial of service attack on the NFS server by capping the count of decoded POSIX ACL entries at a fixed maximum, rather than allowing it to be chosen by the client. The problem arises from an O(n^2) bubble sort used in the server's compound processing path, which can lead to unbounded CPU usage if the client chooses an excessively large count. By rejecting counts greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES before any allocation or sorting occurs, the patch bounds the number of comparisons and prevents the potential denial of service attack.

review_comments.author: Rick Macklem
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Rick Macklem noted that Chuck previously disagreed with limiting the number of ACEs to NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, arguing it was specific to NFSv3 ACL protocol and the RPC message size limit was sufficient; Rick personally agrees with a limit of 1024 but invites Chuck's input.
review_comments.raw_body:
My recollection is that Chuck didn't like this limit. He argued that it was
specific to the NFSv3 ACL protocol and that the limit on the size of a NFSv4
RPC message was sufficient.  I, personally, think that 1024 is a reasonable
limit for # of ACEs, but Chuck can jump in here if he doesn't agree.
(Note that, if user/groups are configured as "#s in the string", a lot of ACEs
fits in a 4Mbyte RPC message and the server file system will also set a limit
although, as you note, that happens after the sort.)

The good news w.r.t. the sort is that they are normally already sorted,
so the bubble sort is normally just a single pass, I think?

rick

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that using NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs introduces technical debt, as this limit is not defined by the NFSv4 protocol itself and should be clearly distinguished from a protocol limit. He suggested defining an implementation-specific constant with a clear name, such as NFSD4_MAX_yada, to indicate it's an NFSD constraint rather than a protocol requirement.
review_comments.raw_body:
The NFSACL protocol limits the number of ACEs in an ACL to NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
(1024). It’s a limit that is defined in the protocol itself.

The NFSv4 protocol sets no similar limit. In particular, NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES
is not a constant defined or used by the NFSv4.x family of protocols IIRC.

Using NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES to cap the number of ACEs in NFSv4 ACLs is a
convenience, but it adds technical debt (slight though it may be).

So… We can define an implementation limit for NFSv4 ACL support in NFSD.
But it shouldn’t be called NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, IMHO. For clarity of
documentation, pick a number (could be 1024) and state in a comment that
it is an NFSD implementation constraint, not a protocol limit. Name the
constant something like NFSD4_MAX_yada to make it clear it is an
implementation limit, not a protocol limit.

(Check if the Linux POSIX ACL implementation already defines and imposes
a similar limit before defining a new one for NFSv4).

Then prepare for an onslaught of complaints that 1024 is just too small :-)


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested an alternative approach to capping ACL entry counts, proposing that they be capped at the same number as those set via NFSACL, and requested a clear explanation for why this is done.
review_comments.raw_body:
A different take on this might be that we want to ensure that ACLs set
via the NFSv4 POSIX ACL extension can always be retrieved via NFSACL.
In that case, capping the ACE count at the same number makes sense.
As long as the reason for this is clearly mentioned.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/10] nfsd: validate symlink target length in NFSv4 CREATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a security vulnerability in the NFSv4 CREATE operation by validating the length of symlink targets to prevent a client from forcing an unbounded kmalloc allocation. The patch rejects oversized targets and returns an error code early, preventing the allocation from occurring. This fix addresses a long-standing issue that was introduced in Linux 2.6.12-rc2.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various bugs and issues in the NFS daemon, including remotely-triggerable panics, use-after-free errors, denial-of-service vulnerabilities, and protocol fixes. The patches were categorized as 'random bugs' but are now being addressed collectively. The set passes basic pynfs smoke testing and includes contributions from multiple authors.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a race condition in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid, agreed to fix it by taking fi_lock around the fi_deleg_file read and replacing BUG_ON with a graceful error return.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about a potential use-after-free bug in the nfsd4 callback handling code, specifically that the per-session teardown path can free a session while rpciod still holds an inflight callback. The author agrees to mirror the two-stage drain already performed by nfsd4_shutdown_callback() and clear clp->cl_cb_session unconditionally on error returns.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Chris Mason pointed out a concurrency issue in nfsd4_end_grace() where two callers can observe grace_ended == false and proceed into nfsd4_record_grace_done(), leading to list corruption and double-free of nfs4_client_reclaim entries. The author agreed that this is a bug, explained the fix using an atomic test-and-set with try_cmpxchg(), and promised to apply it in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd4_end_grace() guards its drain path with a plain bool:

    if (nn->grace_ended)
            return;
    nn->grace_ended = true;

The read and the write are independent, and nothing in struct
nfsd_net serializes them.  At least two contexts can reach this
code with no lock held:

    laundromat path
      laundry_wq kworker
        nfs4_laundromat()
          nfsd4_end_grace()

    RECLAIM_COMPLETE path
      nfsd compound kthread
        nfsd4_reclaim_complete()
          inc_reclaim_complete()
            nfsd4_end_grace()

Both callers can observe grace_ended == false on different CPUs,
both store true, and both proceed into nfsd4_record_grace_done(),
which invokes the active client_tracking_ops->grace_done callback.
For tracking ops that drain reclaim_str_hashtbl (legacy_tracking_ops
via nfsd4_recdir_purge_old, and the cld v1+ ops via
nfsd4_cld_grace_done), grace_done calls nfs4_release_reclaim(),
which walks every bucket of reclaim_str_hashtbl with no lock and
calls nfs4_remove_reclaim_record() (list_del + kfree) on each
entry.  Two concurrent walkers corrupt the list and double-free
every nfs4_client_reclaim.  A concurrent nfsd4_find_reclaim_client()
iterating the same bucket reads through freed memory.

A third call site exists in nfs4_state_start_net() on the
skip_grace startup path, but it runs under nfsd_mutex before any
client has connected and before the laundromat's first delayed
work fires, so it cannot race with the two callers above.

Fix by replacing the read/write pair with try_cmpxchg() so exactly
one caller transitions grace_ended from false to true and proceeds
into the drain; the loser returns immediately.  bool supports
1-byte cmpxchg on all supported architectures, and no lock
ordering changes are needed.

Fixes: 362063a595be ("nfsd: keep a tally of RECLAIM_COMPLETE operations when using nfsdcld")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c | 17 ++++++++++++++---
 1 file changed, 14 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
index f4d12dbcf97b..dc4ac541436f 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c
@@ -7022,12 +7022,23 @@ nfsd4_renew(struct svc_rqst *rqstp, struct nfsd4_compound_state *cstate,
 static void
 nfsd4_end_grace(struct nfsd_net *nn)
 {
-	/* do nothing if grace period already ended */
-	if (nn->grace_ended)
+	bool expected = false;
+
+	/*
+	 * nfsd4_end_grace() can be entered concurrently from the
+	 * laundromat workqueue and from an nfsd compound thread
+	 * handling RECLAIM_COMPLETE.  Without serialization, both
+	 * callers can observe grace_ended==false and proceed into
+	 * nfsd4_record_grace_done().  For tracking ops whose
+	 * grace_done drains reclaim_str_hashtbl, that results in
+	 * list corruption and a double free of every
+	 * nfs4_client_reclaim entry.  Use an atomic test-and-set so
+	 * exactly one caller proceeds.
+	 */
+	if (!try_cmpxchg(&nn->grace_ended, &expected, true))
 		return;
 
 	trace_nfsd_grace_complete(nn);
-	nn->grace_ended = true;
 	/*
 	 * If the server goes down again right now, an NFSv4
 	 * client will still be allowed to reclaim after it comes back up,

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the nfs4_client_to_reclaim() function causing a memory leak and incorrect behavior when folding in new principal hashes into existing reclaim records. The author agreed to restructure the code in v2 to fix these issues, including pairing the stores of cr_princhash.data and .len with smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire() respectively.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for a fix

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that nfsd3_proc_setacl() silently drops the directory's default ACL when the client doesn't send it, and promised to fix this by adding mask checks before calling set_posix_acl().
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd3_proc_setacl() calls set_posix_acl() unconditionally for both
ACL_TYPE_ACCESS and ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, passing argp->acl_access and
argp->acl_default verbatim. The NFSv3 ACL decoder only populates
those pointers when the corresponding mask bit is set:

    nfs3svc_decode_setaclargs()
      if (args->mask & NFS_ACL)    decode into acl_access
      if (args->mask & NFS_DFACL)  decode into acl_default
      /* otherwise the pointer stays NULL (pc_argzero) */

    nfsd3_proc_setacl()
      set_posix_acl(.., ACL_TYPE_ACCESS,  argp->acl_access)
      set_posix_acl(.., ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, argp->acl_default)

set_posix_acl(idmap, dentry, type, NULL) is the VFS "remove this
ACL type" operation. A NULL pointer that means "the client did not
send this arm" is therefore indistinguishable from "the client
asked to remove this ACL". A SETACL with mask=NFS_ACL silently
drops the directory's default ACL; mask=0 drops both.

The sibling nfsd3_proc_getacl() already consults argp->mask before
touching each arm; mirror that in setacl.

Fix by wrapping each set_posix_acl() call in the matching mask bit
check and initializing error to 0 before inode_lock so that a
request with neither bit set leaves the on-disk ACLs untouched and
returns nfs_ok. The out_drop_lock path and the unconditional
posix_acl_release() at out: are preserved; both NULL-tolerate the
skipped arms.

Fixes: a257cdd0e217 ("[PATCH] NFSD: Add server support for NFSv3 ACLs.")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c | 17 +++++++++++------
 1 file changed, 11 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
index e87731380be8..a87f9d7f32be 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs3acl.c
@@ -105,12 +105,17 @@ static __be32 nfsd3_proc_setacl(struct svc_rqst *rqstp)
 
 	inode_lock(inode);
 
-	error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry, ACL_TYPE_ACCESS,
-			      argp->acl_access);
-	if (error)
-		goto out_drop_lock;
-	error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry, ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT,
-			      argp->acl_default);
+	error = 0;
+	if (argp->mask & NFS_ACL) {
+		error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry,
+				      ACL_TYPE_ACCESS, argp->acl_access);
+		if (error)
+			goto out_drop_lock;
+	}
+	if (argp->mask & NFS_DFACL) {
+		error = set_posix_acl(&nop_mnt_idmap, fh->fh_dentry,
+				      ACL_TYPE_DEFAULT, argp->acl_default);
+	}
 
 out_drop_lock:
 	inode_unlock(inode);

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the nfsd_direct_write() function not properly detecting short writes, which can lead to incorrect file offsets and over-reporting of written bytes. The author agrees that the issue exists and proposes fixing it by snapshotting the segment's byte count before writing and comparing it with the actual error returned by vfs_iocb_iter_write().

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the nfsd_vfs_write() function, which was modified to return a possibly modified stable_how value to its callers. The author explained that this change indicates the client does not need a subsequent COMMIT operation, saving a round trip and allowing the client to dispense with cached dirty data as soon as it receives the server's WRITE response.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the decoder introduced in commit 5fc51dfc2eb1 omitted a symmetric check for ACL entry count, agreed to reject wire counts greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES before allocation to bound CPU usage.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>

nfsd4_decode_posixacl() reads a u32 entry count off the wire and passes
it straight to posix_acl_alloc() and sort_pacl_range(). The latter is
an O(n^2) bubble sort, so a client-chosen count drives unbounded CPU in
the server's compound processing path.

    nfsd4_decode_posixacl()
      xdr_stream_decode_u32(&count)       /* uncapped u32 */
      posix_acl_alloc(count, GFP_KERNEL)
      sort_pacl_range(*acl, 0, count - 1) /* O(n^2) bubble sort */

The encoder side in the same file already rejects ACLs whose a_count
exceeds NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES, but the decoder introduced in commit
5fc51dfc2eb1 ("NFSD: Add support for XDR decoding POSIX draft ACLs")
omitted the symmetric check.

Fix by rejecting a wire count greater than NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES with
nfserr_resource, before any allocation, so the sort is bounded by
NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES^2 comparisons.

Fixes: 5fc51dfc2eb1 ("NFSD: Add support for XDR decoding POSIX draft ACLs")
Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-7
Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
---
 fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c | 2 ++
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)

diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
index c6c50c376b23..5469c6c207ba 100644
--- a/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
+++ b/fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c
@@ -448,6 +448,8 @@ nfsd4_decode_posixacl(struct nfsd4_compoundargs *argp, struct posix_acl **acl)
 
 	if (xdr_stream_decode_u32(argp->xdr, &count) < 0)
 		return nfserr_bad_xdr;
+	if (count > NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES)
+		return nfserr_resource;
 
 	*acl = posix_acl_alloc(count, GFP_KERNEL);
 	if (*acl == NULL)

-- 
2.54.0

-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer NeilBrown questioned the necessity of a spinlock in nfsd_file_get(), suggesting that rcu_read_lock() would be sufficient to prevent file freeing while incrementing refcount, and asked for clarification on the '1' parameter in rcu_dereference_protected().
review_comments.raw_body:
Replacing the BUG_ON with a graceful error is certainly sensible and
probably all that is needed to fix the problem.

I cannot see how the spinlock achieves anything.  If ->fi_deleg_file
could become NULL at this point, it can become NULL just before we take
the spinlock.

We do need to be sure the file (if there is one) doesn't get freed while
nfsd_file_get() is incrementing the refcount, but rcu_read_lock() is the
normal tool for that.
In this case we have

  		ls->ls_file = nfsd_file_get(rcu_dereference_protected(fp->fi_deleg_file, 1));

rcu_dereference_protected(...., 1)
which for me is a warning sign.  What does the '1' mean here?
Presumably something that we cannot easily assert with a C condition, in
which case a comment is called for.

Based on the recent commit which added this I'm guessing 
   fi_delegees > 0 guarantees stability

If that is right, then why do we also need a spinlock to guarantee
stability?

Confused.

NeilBrown

via the NFSv4 POSIX ACL extension can always be retrieved via NFSACL.
In that case, capping the ACE count at the same number makes sense.
As long as the reason for this is clearly mentioned.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the XDR (External Data Representation) length calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget(). The function incorrectly calculates the buffer size, leading to potential out-of-bounds writes or memory leaks. The fix involves breaking down each encoded field explicitly and using xdr_align_size() for variable-length opaque fields.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the bug and suggested a fix. The patch was found to be correct and was applied to the nfsd-testing branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Thu, 28 May 2026 10:38:15 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The XDR buffer size calculation in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget() has
> multiple errors that can result in either an out-of-bounds write or
> leaking uninitialized kernel memory to the client:
> 
>  - fh_len doesn't account for XDR padding on the file handle data
>  - uid and gid lengths use "8 + len" but xdr_encode_opaque() actually
>    writes "4 + xdr_align_size(len)" bytes
>  - ds_len omits the flags and stats_collect_hint fields (8 bytes),
>    while len's header constant overestimates by 8 bytes -- these
>    partially cancel but leave a net mismatch
> 
> [...]

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server's XDR (External Data Representation) padding calculation for the GETDEVICEINFO reply. The issue is that the declared length of the da_addr_body field exceeds the actual encoded data by 2-8 bytes, causing stale reply-page content to leak to the client and mis-aligning subsequent version list decodes. The patch uses xdr_align_size() to match the actual encoding of string lengths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup when there's a race between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the 'effective' memcg from the locking function and having the caller use it to set the shrinker bit.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any specific comments or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Reported-by: Sashiko
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  mm/list_lru.c | 26 +++++++++++++++-----------
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by moving it to a separate static inline function and removing its duplicate definition in the code. The shared function is used in both MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the alloc_anon_folio() function by flattening its retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability. The original top-level if statement was buried four levels deep, making it difficult to understand and modify. By moving the success path to a more visible location, the code becomes cleaner and easier to work with. This change is made in preparation for additional code that will be added in the next patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/compaction: cap compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the value returned by compact_gap() to prevent over-reserving memory for compaction, which can lead to unnecessary reclaim and failed compaction suitability checks. The current implementation returns a gap that scales exponentially with order, but is bounded by COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX. By capping this value at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX, the patch aims to improve compaction success rates and reduce unnecessary reclaim on fragmented production hosts.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would lead to unnecessary reclamation due to genuine memory pressure, suggesting that processes may continuously allocate and reclaim without the gap

Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether the reduction in reclaim is due to increased allocation size or a shift to direct reclaim, requesting clarification on the actual cause.

Reviewer Vlastimil expressed skepticism about the patch's A/B test results, questioning why the free memory would suddenly stabilize and improve significantly after capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX.
sentiment_signals: raised a concern about potential performance impact
review_comments.raw_body:
But doesn't that mean there's genuine memory pressure? We're effectively
raising the high watermark by 4 MB, but if processes are continuously
allocating, we'd be reclaiming without the gap as well? Unless the workload
is sized to fit without the gap.

---

Did the extra reclaim just disappear because we allow the allocations to use
4MB more memory? Or it shifted to direct reclaim?

---

Weird that things would improve that much. I would expect the free memory
just to stabilize around the lower gap but then behave similarly. Are we
missing something here?

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that order-9 THP failures were waking up kswapd and identified compact_gap() as a contributing factor, but did not indicate plans for further revision.

Author acknowledged that the patch's performance improvements are consistent across different kernel versions and builds, but did not address any specific technical concerns or provide a clear resolution to previous feedback.

Author addresses Vlastimil's concern about the impact of capping compact_gap() on order-9 THP allocations, explaining that it allows compaction to take over sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP.

Author acknowledged that bursty network allocations can reserve many pageblocks as high atomic, which affects the patch's assumptions about THP-size pages becoming eligible for allocation.

Author is addressing concerns about over-reserving compact_gap() space, proposes to refactor patch to make it reclaim-focused and use a separate function for non-reclaim users.
sentiment_signals: did not commit to fixing it
author provided additional information but no direct response to prior feedback
review_comments.raw_body:
It wasn't actual pressure, but the repetitive order-9 THP failures that were
waking up kswapd. I should make this more clear in the changelog. After
looking into why so much reclaim was occurring though, the compact gap stood
out since it dictates the target amount to reclaim.

---

6.13. Additional benchmarks were done using a recent mm-new build as well,
and they showed similar reductions in reclaim.

---

Specifically in the order-9 case, the reclaim target goes from 1024 to 32.
What the data shows is that capping the gap allows compaction to take over
sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP. Whereas
in the pre-patch state, trying to reclaim the full 2x THP delays compaction.

---

This patch was tested in isolation, but also occurring was the case where
bursty net allocations reserve many pageblocks as high atomic. So as
THP-size pages become eligible, their blocks are reserved before being
allocated as THP.

---

I'm thinking I could reframe this patch as reclaim-focused and use
min(2UL << order, COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX) as a reclaim-only target, while
either leaving the other non-reclaim users of this function alone or
using the 2x form you suggest above. i.e. I can split this function
into a separate reclaim_compact_gap() and use the originally proposed cap.
Thoughts?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned whether the patch's logic for reclaiming memory still uses the high watermark plus compact_gap() threshold, instead of just reclaiming at least compact_gap() pages, and asked if kswapd's work indicates that memory pressure is present.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not address NUMA machines and suggested considering a recent fix (9c9828d3ead6) to prevent over-reclaim for __GFP_THISNODE THP allocations

Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether the patch addresses a potential issue due to lack of hysteresis in compaction suitability checks, noting that high watermark + compact_gap() is required for termination but compaction can find out it's below this threshold due to concurrent allocations. They suggested examining compaction_ready(), which wants high wmark plus gap plus low-min difference.

Reviewer questioned whether capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would improve compaction suitability checks, pointing out that it could potentially worsen the outcome of these checks.
sentiment_signals: asked for confirmation of mglru usage
review_comments.raw_body:
But the "amount to reclaim" is still defined as "reach high watermark +
compact_gap()" and not "reclaim at least compact_gap() pages" right? Or did
I miss something non-obvious.

So if kswapd did any work, it means the memory was consumed (i.e. there was
some memory pressure) and amount of free memory was below high watermark +
compact_gap()?

BTW, are you using mglru here? (probably not)
As that might be different and I'm not so familiar with it.

---

If it's a NUMA machine, we recently found an over-reclaim issue there fixed
by 9c9828d3ead6 ("mm, page_alloc, thp: prevent reclaim for __GFP_THISNODE
THP allocations")

---

So do I understand correctly we might have an issue due to lack of
hysteresis? We require reaching high watermark + compact_gap() to terminate
reclaim, but then compaction can find out we meanwhile dropped below that
(due to concurrent allocations) and it's not suitable again?

However the suitability checks e.g. compaction_zonelist_suitable() are using
min watermark, so that should provide the difference already.
Actually it's low watermark because of __compaction_suitable() adding an
extra low-min gap for costly orders. But still.

I did just notice compaction_ready() might be too strict. It wants
effectivly high wmark plus the gap plus the low-min difference. Is it
perhaps the underlying issue here?

---

Do I understand correctly you want to cap the reclaim target by
COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX but leave e.g. the compaction_suitable() usage as it is?
But wouldn't that mean we'll actually make changes of passing
compaction_suitable() worse?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prevent high atomic reservations for allocations at or below a certain order (PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER). This is done by adding a check in the reserve_highatomic_pageblock function, which skips reserving entire pageblocks for smaller atomic allocations. The goal of this change is to reduce fragmentation and improve the success rate of large memory allocations, particularly for Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on systems with plenty of free memory.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton questioned whether skipping HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 would break anti-fragmentation guarantees, potentially leading to dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or BTRFS.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko asked

: Does skipping the HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 break the
: anti-fragmentation guarantees for these atomic allocations?
: 
: The MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserve protects high-order atomic allocations
: from failing under fragmentation by taking ownership of the entire
: pageblock.
: 
: If order-1 through order-3 atomic allocations fall back to stealing
: pages, but the pageblock remains in its original migratetype, won't
: order-0 non-atomic allocations consume the remaining contiguous space?
: 
: Under memory pressure, this could leave no contiguous blocks for atomic
: allocations to steal.  Because these atomic allocations cannot trigger
: direct reclaim or compaction, they might fail, potentially leading to
: dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or
: BTRFS.
: 
: Could background compaction or khugepaged be used to unreserve
: HighAtomic blocks dynamically instead of disabling the reserve for
: these orders?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner questioned the patch's effectiveness, noting that disabling highatomic reserve for costly orders didn't seem to matter and suggesting that maxing out reserves is unnecessary as highatomic allocations are typically short-lived.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is an interesting patch. A couple of thoughts:

1. You disabled the highatomic reserve for this workload and it didn't
seem to matter. Presumably <costly orders don't need the protection.

2. Maxing out the reserves is odd. ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC allocations will
try reserved space first, and I'd expect things that are commonly
highatomic to be short-lived. Why don't we stop with a couple of
claimed highatomic blocks that get continuously recycled?

3. The impact on THP and compaction success rate is pretty
extreme. How can 1% of memory throw such a wrench into the gears?

Have you tried this with other workloads?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged reviewer's feedback that smaller atomic allocations do not benefit from the high atomic reservation, agreeing that it should be skipped for orders 1-3.

Author acknowledges that smaller atomic allocations do not reserve entire pageblocks, as observed in experiments where there were no failures for orders 1-3 on a fragmented system.

Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch would prevent smaller atomic allocations from being movable, explaining that the patch actually keeps these pageblocks movable and improves compaction

Author addressed a concern that reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not treated as precious resources, explaining that under real memory pressure they are already given up by the kernel's unreserve mechanism.

Author responded to a concern about introducing additional overhead by explaining that the current patch already reduces reclaim and arguing against adding more scanning/overhead/stats.
review_comments.raw_body:
The data included in the changelog supports the claim that the reserve
does not provide a benefit at these orders. Even on fragmented systems,
orders 1-3 have plenty of pages available.

---

In the experiments, there were no failures for orders 1-3 despite a
fragmented system and no reserved pageblocks for these orders.

---

With the patch, these pageblocks stay movable. So if fallback is needed,
moveable pages can still be taken. But the patch actually improves
compaction so contiguous space is increased overall.

---

Reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not currently treated as a precious
resource. Under real memory pressure, the kernel already gives them up -
the unreserve mechanism kicks in and converts the HighAtomic pageblocks
back to their original migrate type.

---

This would call for extra scanning/overhead/stats. The patch reduces
reclaim, for example. A new scanner feels like going in the opposite
direction.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil pointed out that the patch's logic for reserving high atomic pageblocks may not work as intended when an allocation succeeds before entering the slowpath, potentially leading to unnecessary reservation of additional blocks.

Reviewer Vlastimil suggested that instead of skipping high atomic reservations for low orders, the patch should focus on unreserving these reservations more proactively.

Reviewer noted that if all free memory is in HighAtomic blocks, compaction may not be effective and suggested a suitability check might be incorrectly assuming these blocks are usable
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, but if the allocation succeeds before entering slowpath,
ALLOC_NON_BLOCK won't be set.
But reserving another block should mean we already exhausted the reserved ones.
Unreserving is only done when direct reclaim made some progress but failed
to produce a page. But if it works, or kswapd does the job, we won't enter it?

---

Maybe it's some big burst of highatomic allocations that leads to the
reservations and then they stay around "forever"?

If that's the case I think we should be perhaps looking at the unreserving
being done more proactively, rather than limiting things to costly order.

---

Maybe if ~all free memory is in the highatomic blocks, compaction can't be
effective much. Or some suitability check somewhere in reclaim+compaction
wrongly assumes the highatomic blocks are usable, so it won't do the work.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a potential configuration issue and plans to address it by considering pageblock_order in the patch

Author acknowledged that smaller atomic allocations can still lead to high reservation rates due to steady allocation volumes and agreed to reconsider the patch's approach.

Author acknowledges that the patch improves the situation by making high atomic pageblocks available for THP allocations, and notes that these blocks were previously unusable.

Author acknowledged that the patch's benefits may not be immediately apparent on workloads with frequent network allocations, and noted that memory size is also a factor.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right. Although one detail I realize is I should also consider
pageblock_order as well to avoid any config issue.

---

Even though they may be short-lived, the data shows the volume of
allocations is steady enough to keep the reserves maxed out.

---

Looking at the pre-patched high atomic pageblock counts, that's ~300
pageblocks that could've been used for THPs. They become usable after
the patch.

---

No, but the pre-patch symptoms will show up on workloads where net
allocs are frequent enough to keep the high atomic pageblock count up.
Memory size of hosts involved is a factor as well since it's possible for a
majority of order-9 pages to be stuck in high atomic.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that there is no need for the patch's logic to handle the case where the high atomic reservation is not invoked, and awaits further clarification from the reviewer.

Author acknowledges a concern about high atomic reservation at low orders and explains that high frequency net allocations contribute to this issue, promising to add this information to the changelog.

Author is questioning whether reserving full pageblocks for small allocations is necessary, suggesting that coalescing in the buddy path might be sufficient based on the provided data.

Author JP Kobryn addressed a concern about whether the patch's approach to preventing high atomic reservation at or below costly order is correct, explaining that free highatomic pages are removed from usable free count in __zone_watermark_ok() and compaction suitability path, and that fragmentation accounting may be skewed. Author believes the patch prevents the problem up front.
review_comments.raw_body:
There was just no real pressure to invoke the unreserving. Let me know
if I'm misunderstanding the question.

---

I should add to the changelog the missing info that high frequency
net allocations are responsible for these high atomic reservations.
Even though the allocations are not necessarily long-lived, the
pageblocks remain high atomic.

---

What are your thoughts if we instead look at it as: should we be reserving
full pageblocks for small allocations?

It seems to come down to whether we want the disproportionate protection 
of full
pageblocks (below costly order) for high atomic allocs vs letting them 
coalesce
in the buddy path. Is the data not enough to justify the latter?

---

I could be missing something, but I spent some time tonight looking into
this and didn't find an issue in the compaction/reclaim suitability path.

__compaction_suitable() calls __zone_watermark_ok(), and that path
subtracts free MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC pages from usable free memory for
callers without reserve access:

 /*
  * If the caller does not have rights to reserves below the min
  * watermark then subtract the free pages reserved for highatomic.
  */
 if (likely(!(alloc_flags & ALLOC_RESERVES)))
   unusable_free += READ_ONCE(z->nr_free_highatomic);

So free highatomic pages are removed from the usable free count there.

Also, the suitable-free-block check in __zone_watermark_ok() only treats
MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC as usable when alloc_flags includes
ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC (or ALLOC_OOM). __compaction_suitable() passes
ALLOC_CMA here (not ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC), so I don't think compaction is
incorrectly treating free highatomic blocks as usable.

The only caveat I noticed is the fragmentation accounting side:
fill_contig_page_info() / fragmentation_index() appear to count
free_area[order].nr_free across migratetypes, so fragmentation scoring
may look better than they really are. But that seems adjacent
to this patch.

I think though that by the time we consider reclaim or compaction we're
dealing with the aftermath. The patch prevents the problem from occurring
up front.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that high atomic allocations will attempt to reuse already reserved blocks in the fastpath, which may not be ideal, but they will still try them before reserving another block.

Reviewer noted that high atomic reservations are made at the pageblock level, which can lead to scattered reservations across multiple pageblocks, and suggested a grouping approach

Reviewer Vlastimil (SUSE) expressed concern that the patch effectively kills the highatomic feature, as evidenced by zero costly highatomic allocation attempts and no increase in net receive fallbacks. They suggested keeping the reservations working for non-costly orders and proposed two directions to try: modifying the fastpath __alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() function or making unreserving more aggressive.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry, it was more thinking out loud about Johannes' point than a question.
Yeah it seems there was no real pressure to invoke unreserving.

The reserving side is probably fine. Highatomic allocation will not try the
already reserved blocks in he fastpath, which is maybe not ideal. But they
will try them before reserving another block, and that's the important part.

---

Well, since migratetypes operate on the pageblock level, so do the
highatomic reservations. It at least groups them together and not scatter
all over random pageblocks?

---

But I think as a result the highatomic feature is effectively dead. Your
results confirm there are no more Highatomic pageblocks and zero Atomic
order-4+ allocations (actually it's weird there's still 1 highatomic
pageblock with zero allocations that would reserve it, or is that a rounding
error due to calculating average across multiple hosts?).

I think it's not a surprise that there are no costly highatomic allocation
attempts, we've always said they are too easy to fail, so likely nobody even
tries them. MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC was introduced by Mel [1] and evaluated on
order-1. Even the non-costly orders can fail of course and should have
fallbacks, highatomic reserves are just supposed to make the success more
likely as that improves e.g. the networking receive performance, and they do
use non-costly orders.

Did you observe no increase of net receive fallbacks due to this patch?
Would that be an universal outcome? I.e. did highatomic reservations become
obsolete thanks to other improvements to the page allocator since they were
introduced? That would be great as we could remove it completely and
simplify the code, but we don't know that yet.

If there are still benefits, they probably should stay, but that means keep
them working for non-costly orders, and we should fix the observed problems
differently. I can see two directions to try in that order.

- You say there are "high frequency net allocations" so I assume they are
ongoing. We could try modify the fastpath __alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() to
properly evaluate ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC and let them prefer the reserved blocks
in cases that do not end up in __alloc_pages_slowpath(). This should ensure
the reserved blocks are actually being used even if we are above low
watermarks and don't enter the slowpath.

- If that doesn't help and we still have unused highatomic pageblocks,
figure out how that happens - is the highatomic allocation frequency higher
at some point, resulting in their increase, and then it drops and they stay
around? If yes, think about how to make the unreserving more aggressive than
it currently is.

[1]
https://lore.kernel.org/all/1442832762-7247-10-git-send-email-mgorman@techsingularity.net/

review_comments.author: Frank Linden
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned whether the issue was reproducible without the patch, specifically asking if the symptoms were the same when including a different commit (b480cbb07102) that also affects high atomic reservation
review_comments.raw_body:
Was this issue reproduced with a tree that does not have your patch,
but includes b480cbb07102 ("mm/page_alloc: don't increase highatomic
reserve after pcp alloc") ? The symptoms here seem the same.

- Frank

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling
series_items.patch_summary: The patch fixes an off-by-one error in the port I/O handling code for TDX (Trusted Execution Environment) on x86 architecture. The issue arises from the use of GENMASK macro, which includes one extra bit when calculating the mask size. This causes incorrect results for certain I/O operations. The fix adjusts the calculation to exclude the last bit.

review_comments.author: Edgecombe, Rick P
review_comments.summary: Applied the Reviewed-by tag without commenting on the patch's technical merits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the x86/tdx code where 32-bit port I/O operations do not correctly zero-extend the result to 64 bits. The current implementation only masks the lower 32 bits, preserving the upper 32 bits of RAX when emulating a 32-bit port IN instruction. To fix this, the patch updates the handle_in function to clear the entire RAX register when the I/O size is 4 bytes, ensuring correct zero-extension for larger sizes. For smaller sizes (1 or 2 bytes), the upper bits are preserved as before. This change ensures that x86/tdx correctly handles 32-bit port I/O operations and maintains architectural compliance.

review_comments.author: Edgecombe, P
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch's zero-extension fix might be unnecessary or cause issues if the GHCI spec is amended to require host or guest responsibility for this operation, and suggested merging it anyway but acknowledged a small risk of complications.
review_comments.raw_body:
We are working on getting the GHCI spec amended to clarify who is supposed to do
this zero-extending and masking, host or guest. For this and the similar

to merge this, but there is some small risk of complications depending on how
that discussion goes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/18] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() function, which is responsible for setting the UFFD_WP bit on present HugeTLB PTEs. The issue arises when the kernel-owned snapshot of the PTE is not updated atomically with the hardware update, leading to stale data being written back over the live hardware bits. To fix this, the patch mirrors the pattern used in the non-hugetlb sibling make_uffd_wp_pte() function by using ptep_modify_prot_start() and ptep_modify_prot_commit(). This ensures that the PTE is updated atomically, preventing the race condition and ensuring data consistency.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/18] mm/huge_memory: preserve pmd_swp_uffd_wp on device-private PMD downgrade
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's huge memory handling code, specifically when downgrading a device-private PMD (Page Map Directory) to a non-device-private one. The issue was that the pmd_swp_uffd_wp bit was being lost during this downgrade process, which could cause UFFD_WP (User Fault on File Descriptor Write Protection) traps to be bypassed. To fix this, the patch adds code to preserve the pmd_swp_uffd_wp bit when downgrading a device-private PMD.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/18] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the userfaultfd code to gate the writability check on pte_present() when checking if a fault still requires userspace handling due to write protection (WP). This prevents incorrect results from non-marker swap entries, which can occur during concurrent migration or swap-out. The change ensures that the lockless re-check only inspects present PTE bits when the entry is actually present, preventing potential deadlocks and ensuring correct behavior in such scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/18] mm: skip out-of-range bits in mk_vma_flags()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel where passing out-of-range bits to the mk_vma_flags() function could silently add extra bits to the mask, potentially causing issues. The patch introduces VMA_NO_BIT as a special value that is used when a bit number is out of range, and modifies the DECLARE_VMA_BIT macro to use this value for invalid bit numbers. This ensures that only valid bits are added to the mask, preventing potential problems.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/18] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to use them independently of NUMA balancing. The six architectures currently supporting protnone PTEs select this option and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it. No functional changes are made, but the infrastructure is now available separately from NUMA balancing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/18] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' across various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The change is purely mechanical and does not alter any behavior. This is done in preparation for userfaultfd RWP (Read-Write Protection) to use the same bit to mark access-tracking PTEs, distinct from mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA-hinting PTEs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/18] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and variables related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs in addition to write protection. The renaming is mechanical and does not change behavior. The changes affect various architecture-specific files as well as core mm code. The goal of this patch is to simplify the kernel's handling of userfaultfd by making its internal names consistent with its new usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/18] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP extends the existing write-protection mechanism to also trap reads on protected ranges. The patch introduces the necessary infrastructure, including helpers and documentation updates, but does not yet set or query the flag. It is a preparatory patch for the rest of the series, which will implement userfaultfd read-write protection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/18] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. The first flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, which distinguishes it from a plain mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting fault. The second flag restores the original vma->vm_page_prot and clears the uffd bit. These flags are used for userfaultfd RWP (Read-Write Protection) and are wired through various change_protection() functions to ensure consistent semantics across different memory types, including anonymous, shmem, THP, and hugetlb.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/18] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages with PAGE_NONE semantics. The patch adds checks and modifications to maintain the uffd bit (RWP marker) across these operations, ensuring that it is not lost during PTE rewrites.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/18] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP, ensuring that per-PTE RWP state is not silently destroyed or bypassed. The changes affect khugepaged, rmap, and GUP, which are modified to handle VM_UFFD_RWP ranges with the same protection as VM_UFFD_WP. This patch addresses a problem where userfaultfd RWP markers were being ignored in certain scenarios, leading to potential data corruption or security issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/18] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd (UFFD) system, which allows userspace to track read-write protection on memory ranges. It introduces two new UFFD ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, as well as a capability bit UFFD_FEATURE_RWP. The feature is not yet advertised in the UAPI, but will be exposed in a follow-up patch. The patch also includes changes to the kernel's configuration, documentation, and code to support this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/18] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) to the userfaultfd interface in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new fault delivery mechanism that triggers when a process attempts to access memory protected by RWP, and exposes a new UAPI command to register RWP mode. The patch also updates various kernel functions to handle RWP faults correctly. The goal is to provide userspace with a way to track and manage read-write protection for specific memory regions. The approach taken involves modifying the fault handling paths in the kernel to detect RWP-protected pages and dispatching UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP messages through the userfaultfd interface. This allows userspace to resolve RWP faults using the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT command.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/18] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for tracking read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd. The existing PTE bit is reused for both write-protected and RWP-protected pages, but this patch introduces a distinction between the two by scoping each to the relevant VMA. This change also updates the pagemap_hugetlb_category function to take into account the VMA when determining which category to use. The patch aims to provide a more accurate representation of page state and improve the userfaultfd interface.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/18] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines, where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this feature upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when possible, preventing unnecessary copy-on-write faults.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/18] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This patch also includes changes to userfaultfd.c and include/uapi/linux/userfaultfd.h files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/18] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork operations. They verify that RWP works correctly in different situations, such as when pages are touched, accessed, or protected. The patch includes 11 test cases that run against anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types. It also ensures that the userfaultfd API returns the expected ioctls bitmask. The tests aim to provide comprehensive coverage for UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/18] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical workflow for VMM working-set-tracking from detection to eviction and back to async mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces working set tracking for VM guest memory using the userfaultfd (UFFD) mechanism. The first patch in the series fixes a race condition in the make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() function, which is responsible for setting the UFFD_WP bit on present HugeTLB PTEs. The fix involves mirroring the pattern used by the non-hugetlb sibling function to ensure atomicity and consistency of the update process. This patch series aims to improve memory management in VM guests by providing a more accurate view of their working set, which is essential for efficient resource allocation and optimization.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the RWP (Read-Write Protect) mode and its interaction with NUMA balancing, specifically that the disarm/rebuild paths in patches 10/18 and 13/18 should be gated on pte_uffd() to prevent PROT_NONE markers from being lost. The author agrees to restructure these patches in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

This series adds userfaultfd support for tracking the working set of
VM guest memory, so a VMM can identify cold pages and evict them to
tiered or remote storage.

v1: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260427114607.4068647-1-kas@kernel.org/
v2: https://lore.kernel.org/all/cover.1778254670.git.kas@kernel.org/
v3: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260522133857.552279-1-kirill@shutemov.name/
v4: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260525113737.1942478-1-kas@kernel.org/

== Changes since v4 ==

v5 mainly addresses Sashiko AI review feedback on v4.

  - Patches 1-4 are new pre-existing fixes surfaced by that review
    (each carries Fixes:/Cc: stable@).
  - 05/18: LoongArch select of ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE gated on 64BIT.
  - 10/18, 13/18: gate RWP disarm/rebuild paths on pte_uffd() so
    NUMA-balancing PROT_NONE markers survive.
  - 12/18: reject UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP on PROT_NONE VMAs.
  - 14/18: PM_SCAN_WP_MATCHING on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA silently skips
    instead of -EINVAL, preserving the atomic read-and-reset.
  - 16/18: UFFDIO_SET_MODE feature check goes through
    userfaultfd_features() for KCSAN clean read.
  - 17/18: drop _UFFDIO_SET_MODE from the baseline UFFDIO_API check
    so the test still passes on older kernels.
  - 18/18: VMM example switches to sync before PAGEMAP_SCAN; names
    uffdio_api so callers can read back negotiated features; fixes
    a couple of stray identifiers in the eviction loop.

113/113 of tools/testing/selftests/mm/uffd-unit-tests pass (46 new
RWP cases + existing UFFD groups, no regressions from patches 1-4).

== Problem ==

A VMM managing guest memory needs to:

  1. detect which pages are still being touched (working-set
     tracking);
  2. safely evict cold pages to slower tiered or remote storage;
  3. fetch them back on demand when accessed again.

== Approach ==

UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP is a new userfaultfd registration mode, in
parallel with the existing MODE_MISSING / MODE_WP / MODE_MINOR. It
uses the same mechanism on every backing -- anon, shmem, hugetlbfs:

  - PAGE_NONE on the PTE (the same primitive NUMA balancing uses)
    makes the page inaccessible while keeping it resident;
  - the uffd PTE bit (the one MODE_WP already owns) marks the entry
    as "userfaultfd-tracked" so the protnone fault path can tell an
    RWP fault apart from an mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting
    fault.

VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP are mutually exclusive per VMA, so the
same PTE bit safely carries both meanings depending on the
registered VMA flag.

In sync mode, the kernel delivers a UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP message
to the registered handler, and the handler resolves the fault with
UFFDIO_RWPROTECT clearing MODE_RWP. In async mode
(UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC), the fault is auto-resolved in-place: the
kernel restores the original PTE permissions and the faulting thread
continues without a userfaultfd message ever being delivered.
Userspace then learns which pages were touched by reading
PAGE_IS_ACCESSED out of PAGEMAP_SCAN -- pages whose uffd bit is
still set were not re-accessed since the last RWP cycle.

UFFDIO_RWPROTECT is the protect/unprotect ioctl, mirroring
UFFDIO_WRITEPROTECT.

UFFDIO_SET_MODE flips RWP_ASYNC <-> sync at runtime under
mmap_write_lock() + vma_start_write(), so a VMM can run in async
mode for detection and switch to sync for race-free eviction without
re-registering the userfaultfd.

== Typical VMM workflow ==

  /* arm */
  UFFDIO_API(features = RWP | RWP_ASYNC)
  UFFDIO_REGISTER(MODE_RWP)

  /* detection cycle (async) */
  UFFDIO_RWPROTECT(range, RWP)
  sleep(interval)

  /* freeze the cold snapshot before scanning */
  UFFDIO_SET_MODE(disable = RWP_ASYNC)                  /* sync */
  PAGEMAP_SCAN(!PAGE_IS_ACCESSED) -> cold pages

  /* eviction (sync mode traps races) */
  pwrite(cold) + fallocate(FALLOC_FL_PUNCH_HOLE, cold)
  UFFDIO_WAKE(cold)
  UFFDIO_SET_MODE(enable  = RWP_ASYNC)                  /* resume */

== Series layout ==

Patches 1 to 4 are independent pre-existing fixes (Fixes:/Cc: stable@)
that the RWP code shares paths with -- they can be picked separately
if needed:

  1: fs/proc/task_mmu: huge make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race
     -- missing huge_ptep_modify_prot_start() can lose hardware
     Dirty/Accessed updates.
  2: mm/huge_memory: change_non_present_huge_pmd() drops
     pmd_swp_uffd_wp on the writable -> readable device-private
     PMD rewrite; plain mprotect() silently strips the marker.
  3: userfaultfd: must_wait() applies pte_write() to a locklessly
     read PTE without checking pte_present() -- swap/migration
     entries decode random offset bits and the thread can stay
     parked on a stale fault.
  4: mm: mk_vma_flags() OOBs into the first word of vma_flags_t on
     32-bit when called with a bit >= BITS_PER_LONG. Harmless by
     coincidence today (the wraparound lands on a bit that's
     already in the mask), but any future high-numbered bit would
     silently corrupt the result. Add VMA_NO_BIT and skip negative
     bits in DECLARE_VMA_BIT().

Patches 5 to 7 are preparatory:

  5: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING.
  6-7: rename _PAGE_BIT_UFFD_WP, pte_uffd_wp() and friends to drop
       the _WP suffix, since the bit now carries WP and RWP meaning
       depending on the VMA flag. The SCAN_PTE_UFFD enum's ftrace
       output string is intentionally kept as "pte_uffd_wp" so
       trace-based tooling does not silently break.

Patches 8 to 11 add the in-kernel mechanism:

  8: VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag (aliased to VM_NONE until 12/18 introduces
     CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP together with the UAPI).
  9: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() primitive (PAGE_NONE +
     uffd bit, plus a RESOLVE counterpart).
 10: marker preservation across swap, device-exclusive, migration,
     fork, mremap, UFFDIO_MOVE, hugetlb copy, and mprotect().
 11: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP.

Patches 12 to 16 wire the userspace surface:

  12: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
      (introduces CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP).
  13: RWP fault delivery and exposure of UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP.
  14: PAGE_IS_ACCESSED in PAGEMAP_SCAN.
  15: UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution.
  16: UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle.

Patches 17 and 18 are kernel tests and Documentation/. Matching
userfaultfd(2) and ioctl_userfaultfd(2) man-page updates will be
sent as a separate patchset against the kernel.org linux-man tree.

Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) (18):
  fs/proc/task_mmu: fix make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race
  mm/huge_memory: preserve pmd_swp_uffd_wp on device-private PMD
    downgrade
  userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()
  mm: skip out-of-range bits in mk_vma_flags()
  mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
  mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
  mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
  mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
  mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
  mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
  mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
  userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT
    plumbing
  mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose
    UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
  mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
  userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
  userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
  selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
  Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
